title: Review & Strengthen
narrativeIntro: Tomorrow is the last day. Today, you revisit. The Drift shows you where your understanding is brightest and where it's still fragile. Each challenge today is a synthesis — a concept from an earlier week, revisited with everything you now know. Stronger foundations make for a stronger finish.
narrativeOutro: Solid. You can feel it. Not just knowledge but fluency — the difference between knowing the words and speaking the language. Tomorrow, you prove it.
phmConnectionNote: Before the final mission, Ryland reviews everything, ensures every system is solid. You review and strengthen weak areas.
conceptsPreview: Cross-week synthesis — JS foundations through the Angular lens

challenges.title: Scope Inside a Service
challenges.narrativeHook: A service method uses a callback. The callback loses `this`. The bug is week-one — spotted in week-four code.
challenges.instructions:
The `TimerService.start()` uses `setInterval` with a regular function. Inside the callback, `this.count` is `undefined`.

Fix the scoping issue — the same problem as Day 11's `this` challenge, now appearing inside an Angular service.

files.name: timer.service.ts

tests.description: After start() and 300ms, count should be >= 2
tests.testCode: const s = new TimerService(); s.start(); await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 350)); s.stop(); if (s.count < 2) throw new Error(`count should be >= 2 after 350ms, got ${s.count} — 'this' may still be wrong`);

const s = new TimerService(); s.start(); await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 150)); s.stop(); const countAtStop = s.count; await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 200)); if (s.count !== countAtStop) throw new Error('count should not increase after stop()');

explainGate.prompt: Why does a regular function inside setInterval lose `this`, and why does an arrow function solve it? How does this connect to Day 11's lesson?

hints: This is the same bug from Day 11 — regular functions define their own 'this', arrow functions inherit it.
Arrow functions capture 'this' from the enclosing scope (the class method), so this.count refers to the service instance.

challenges.title: Retry Logic
challenges.narrativeHook: The first attempt fails. Try again. And again. Stop after three failures.
challenges.instructions:
Write a `retry(fn, times)` function:

- `fn` is an async function that may reject
- `times` is the max number of attempts
- Returns the resolved value if any attempt succeeds
- Throws the last error if all attempts fail
- Does not delay between attempts

files.name: retry.ts

tests.description: retry should return value on first success
tests.testCode: const result = await retry(() => Promise.resolve('ok'), 3); if (result !== 'ok') throw new Error(`Should return 'ok', got '${result}'`);

tests.description: retry should succeed on second attempt if first fails
tests.testCode: let calls = 0; const fn = () => { calls++; return calls === 1 ? Promise.reject('fail') : Promise.resolve('success'); }; const r = await retry(fn, 3); if (r !== 'success') throw new Error(`Should succeed on 2nd attempt, got '${r}'`);

tests.description: retry should throw last error after all attempts fail
tests.testCode: let caught = null; try { await retry(() => Promise.reject('always-fails'), 3); } catch (e) { caught = e; } if (caught !== 'always-fails') throw new Error(`Should throw last error, got '${caught}'`);

tests.description: retry should attempt exactly 'times' times before giving up
tests.testCode: let calls = 0; try { await retry(() => { calls++; return Promise.reject('x'); }, 4); } catch(e) {} if (calls !== 4) throw new Error(`Should attempt exactly 4 times, got ${calls}`);

hints: The `<T>` in the function signature is a TypeScript generic — it means the function works with any return type. You don't need to understand it fully to solve this challenge; just implement the retry logic.
Use a for loop from 0 to times. Inside, wrap each attempt in try/catch.
On success (no throw), return the value immediately: return await fn();
After the loop, throw the last caught error: throw lastError;

challenges.title: Closure in a Loop
challenges.narrativeHook: A loop creates functions. Each function references `i`. What does each one log?
challenges.instructions:
A `for` loop creates three async functions that log `i`. Each runs after a short delay.

Predict what each logs.
challenges.solution:
// No code changes — this is a prediction challenge.
// Answer: 3, 3, 3 — var is function-scoped, all callbacks share the same i which is 3 by the time they run.
challenges.predictPrompt: What does each setTimeout callback log?

explainGate.prompt: Why do all three callbacks log 3, and how would changing `var` to `let` fix this?

hints: var is function-scoped — there is only ONE variable i shared across all iterations.
By the time any setTimeout runs (after the loop), i is already 3.
let creates a new block-scoped binding per iteration — each callback would capture its own i.

challenges.title: Pipeline
challenges.narrativeHook: Five tools. One pipeline. Data enters raw, exits processed. Build the chain.
challenges.instructions:
Complete the `DataPipeline.run(ids)` method:

1. For each id, call `fetchItem(id)` — runs in parallel (use `Promise.all`)
2. Filter out any rejected results (use `Promise.allSettled` instead)
3. Map successful results: `{ id, label: item.name.toUpperCase() }`
4. Sort by `label` ascending
5. Return the sorted array

`fetchItem` rejects for id `99`.

files.name: data-pipeline.ts

tests.testCode: const p = new DataPipeline(); const r = await p.run([0, 1, 2]); if (r.length !== 3) throw new Error(`Expected 3 items, got ${r.length}`);
const p = new DataPipeline(); const r = await p.run([0, 99, 1]); if (r.length !== 2) throw new Error(`id 99 should be excluded, expected 2 items, got ${r.length}`);
const p = new DataPipeline(); const r = await p.run([0, 1]); if (r.some(i => i.label !== i.label.toUpperCase())) throw new Error('All labels should be uppercase');

tests.description: items should be sorted by label ascending
tests.testCode: const p = new DataPipeline(); const r = await p.run([0, 1, 2, 3]); for (let i = 1; i < r.length; i++) { if (r[i].label < r[i-1].label) throw new Error(`Not sorted: ${r[i-1].label} before ${r[i].label}`); }

hints: Use Promise.allSettled instead of Promise.all — it never rejects, returning 'fulfilled' or 'rejected' for each.
Map to the shape: .map(r => ({ id: r.value.id, label: r.value.item.name.toUpperCase() }))

explainGate.prompt: What is the difference between Promise.all and Promise.allSettled, and why is allSettled the right choice when some requests might fail?
